1: YHWH mālākh gēʾût lābēsh — the LORD reigns! He is clothed with majesty. YHWH mālākh (the LORD reigns/has become king) — the enthronement proclamation formula that opens the cluster of enthronement psalms (93, 95-99). Whether mālākh is timeless-present (always reigns) or a punctual past (has just become king in a liturgical ceremony) has been much debated; the proclamation asserts both eternal sovereignty and its immediate recognition. Gēʾût lābēsh (clothed in majesty — from gāʾāh: to rise, be exalted; the swelling, surging of divine glory). lābēsh YHWH ʿōz hitʾazzār — the LORD is dressed in strength and has girded himself. Hitʾazzār (hitpael of ʿāzar: to gird oneself — the warrior's preparation). ʾap-tikkôn tēbēl bal-timmôṭ — the world is firmly established — it cannot be shaken. Tikkôn (niphal of kûn: to be established, fixed) — creation's stability as the consequence of divine sovereignty.
2: nākhôn kissĕʾăkhā mēʾāz — your throne has been established from ancient times. Nākhôn (established, fixed — the same root as tikkôn in v1) — the divine throne's stability mirrors the world's stability: both fixed by the same sovereign. Mēʾāz (from of old, from long ago — the deep past; cf. Micah 5:2's mîqedem mîmê ʿôlām). mēʿôlām ʾāttāh — you yourself are from everlasting. Mēʿôlām (from eternity, from the indefinitely ancient past) — YHWH's own existence predates the throne he occupies and the world it governs; the most compressed statement of divine eternity in the psalm.
3: nāśĕʾû nĕhārôt YHWH nāśĕʾû nĕhārôt qôlām — the great waters have lifted up their voice, LORD; the rushing floods have lifted up their voice. The triple repetition of nāśĕʾû nĕhārôt (the rivers/floods have lifted up) mimics the rising surging of waters — the anaphora performs the flood's insistent swelling. yiśśĕʾû nĕhārôt dākyām — and their crashing waves. Dākî (crashing, pounding waves — from dākhāh: to crush, pound). The chaos-waters making themselves heard — the cosmic challenge to divine sovereignty that v4 answers.
4: miqqōlôt mayim rabbîm — mightier than the roar of many waters. Mayim rabbîm (many waters — the chaotic primordial flood; cf. Revelation 17:1 where many waters are the nations) — the most powerful force in the ancient Near Eastern cosmological imagination. ʾaddîrîm mishbĕrê-yām — mightier than the waves of the sea. Mishbār (breaker, crashing wave) of the sea — the sea in its most violent aspect. ʾaddîr bammarôm YHWH — the LORD on high is mighty. ʾAddîr (mighty, majestic — the great-power word) placed both at the beginning (waters mighty) and the end (LORD mighty) — the seas' power compared to YHWH's. YHWH's power in the high place (bammarôm) exceeds both. The cosmic battle is over before it begins.
5: ʿēdōtekhā neʾĕmnû mĕʾōd — your decrees are completely trustworthy. ʿĒdâh (testimonies, decrees — the divine statutes as witness to YHWH's character; cf. Psalms 19:7). Neʾĕmnû (niphal of ʾāman: they are reliable, trustworthy — the root of ʾāmēn). The moral order (ʿēdôt: the divine testimony-law) matches the physical order (the world established, v1) — both secured by the same divine sovereignty. lĕbêtekhā naʾăwāh-qōdesh YHWH lĕʾōrekh yāmîm — holiness befits your house, LORD, forevermore. Nāʾāh (to be fitting, to become, to adorn) — holiness (qōdesh) adorns/befits the divine house as gēʾût adorns YHWH in v1. The psalm closes with the moral attribute (holiness) answering the cosmic attribute (majesty): the King who defeats the chaos-waters is also the Holy One whose house is adorned with holiness.
